text: To date, achieving both accurate drug delivery and high drug loading remains a major challenge in cancer chemotherapy. Self-assembling drug delivery systems (DSDSs) have emerged as a new platform of nanocarriers for improving drug delivery, yet achieving long-term supramolecular stability in the in vivo environment is highly challenging. Here, we designed a light-activated anticancer palladium complex, PdL, which self-assembles into nanostructures through the assistance of metallophilic Pd···Pd interactions at the in vivo environment confirmed by both theoretical calculations and experiments. PdL-derived self-assembled nanoparticles (NPs) showed a remarkably long blood circulation time of over 12 h and reached as high as 10.2% injected dose per gram (ID/g) in human skin melanoma (A375) xenograft tumors. Upon activation with green light, PdL-induced a strong type I photodynamic effect and produced large amounts of reactive oxygen species (ROS), even under both normoxic and hypoxic conditions. In vitro, PdL exhibited up to a 96-fold enhancement of anticancer activity against A375 cells, while in vivo, PdL-induced significant tumor killing. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of using the metallophilic self-assembly approach to prepare stable and high-loading supramolecular nanotherapeutics with superior tumor-targeting efficacy.
explanation: The text is highly polished and contains many plausible, specific experimental details; such a style can be generated by AI models trained on scientific abstracts, making AI-authorship plausible though not certain.
